label: SQL Server
description: Connect to Microsoft SQL Server or Azure SQL Database using host, port, credentials, and optional SSL encryption.

form.title: Connect SQL Server
form.subtitle: Enter your SQL Server or Azure SQL connection details to start querying your database.

methods.label: SQL Server Authentication
methods.description: Connect with a SQL Server login (username + password). Works for on-premises SQL Server, Azure SQL Database, and Azure SQL Managed Instance.
methods.how_to:
## SQL Server Authentication

The simplest path: provide your server details and a SQL login.

### Steps

1. Find your server hostname or IP. For Azure SQL this looks like `yourserver.database.windows.net`; for on-prem it may be a hostname or IP address.
2. Confirm the port — SQL Server defaults to **1433**. Named instances may use a dynamic port; check SQL Server Configuration Manager → SQL Server Network Configuration → Protocols → TCP/IP → IP Addresses.
3. Ensure TCP/IP is enabled in SQL Server Configuration Manager (on-prem) or that your Azure SQL firewall allows your IP (Azure Portal → SQL Server → Networking → Firewall rules).
4. Confirm the SQL login exists and has access to the target database: `SELECT name FROM sys.sql_logins;`
5. Fill in the fields below. Enable **Encrypt connection (SSL)** unless your network is fully private — Azure SQL requires it.

### Gotchas

- Azure SQL always requires encryption; leave the toggle on.
- If you use a named instance (e.g. `HOST\SQLEXPRESS`), put the hostname in **Host** and leave **Port** at 1433 — or configure a fixed port for that instance.
- `sa` login works but is discouraged; create a least-privilege login instead.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Host
fields.description: Hostname or IP address of your SQL Server. For Azure SQL, copy from the Azure Portal → SQL Database → Overview → Server name.

fields.name: port
fields.label: Port
fields.description: TCP port SQL Server listens on. Default is 1433.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database
fields.description: Name of the database to connect to.

fields.name: username
fields.label: Username
fields.description: SQL Server login name. For Azure SQL this is often in the format user@servername.

fields.name: password
fields.label: Password
fields.description: Password for the SQL Server login.

fields.name: encrypt
fields.label: Encrypt connection (SSL)
fields.description: Enable TLS encryption. Required for Azure SQL; recommended for all connections.

fields.name: trust_server_certificate
fields.label: Trust server certificate
fields.description: Skip SSL certificate validation. Only enable on private/dev networks with self-signed certs — never in production.

methods.label: Windows / Active Directory Authentication
methods.description: Authenticate using a Windows domain account or Azure Active Directory credentials. Suitable for on-premises SQL Server joined to a domain or Azure SQL with AAD integration.
methods.how_to:
## Windows / Active Directory Authentication

Use a domain account (on-prem) or an Azure Active Directory user (Azure SQL).

### On-premises Windows Authentication

1. Your SQL Server must be configured to allow Windows Authentication (mixed or Windows-only mode).
2. Provide the domain and username in the format `DOMAIN\username` or just `username` if the machine is already on the domain.
3. Ensure the Windows account has been granted access in SQL Server: `CREATE LOGIN [DOMAIN\user] FROM WINDOWS;`

### Azure SQL with AAD Password Authentication

1. In the Azure Portal, confirm your SQL Server has an **Azure Active Directory admin** set (SQL Server → Azure Active Directory).
2. The connecting user must exist as a contained database user: `CREATE USER [user@domain.com] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER;`
3. Enter the AAD username (email) and password in the fields below and set **Authentication type** to `azure-active-directory-password`.

### Gotchas

- Kerberos-based Windows Auth requires the client machine to be domain-joined; Anton running on a non-domain machine will fall back to NTLM or fail.
- For Azure SQL, prefer SQL auth or AAD with MFA via OAuth rather than AAD password auth where possible.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Host
fields.description: Hostname or IP address of the SQL Server.

fields.name: port
fields.label: Port
fields.description: TCP port SQL Server listens on. Default is 1433.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database
fields.description: Name of the database to connect to.

fields.name: auth_type
fields.label: Authentication type
fields.description: Choose Windows domain authentication for on-prem, or AAD Password for Azure SQL.

fields.name: domain
fields.label: Domain
fields.description: Windows domain name (NTLM only). Leave blank if not applicable.

fields.name: username
fields.label: Username
fields.description: Windows username (without domain prefix) or AAD email address.

fields.name: encrypt
fields.label: Encrypt connection (SSL)
fields.description: Enable TLS encryption. Required for Azure SQL.

fields.name: trust_server_certificate
fields.label: Trust server certificate
fields.description: Skip SSL certificate validation. Only enable on private/dev networks with self-signed certs.

methods.label: Connection String
methods.description: Paste a full ADO.NET or JDBC connection string. Handy when you already have one from Azure Portal or your DBA.
methods.how_to:
## Connection String

Paste a pre-built connection string directly — useful when your Azure Portal, DBA, or existing app config already has one ready.

### Where to find it

**Azure SQL (Azure Portal)**
1. Open the Azure Portal → navigate to your SQL Database.
2. In the left menu, click **Connection strings**.
3. Copy the **ADO.NET** string. It looks like:
   `Server=tcp:yourserver.database.windows.net,1433;Initial Catalog=mydb;User ID=myuser;Password=mypassword;Encrypt=True;`
4. Replace `{your_password}` placeholder if present.

**On-premises SQL Server**
Build it manually:
`Server=myhost,1433;Database=mydb;User Id=myuser;Password=mypassword;Encrypt=True;`

### Gotchas

- Do not commit connection strings with passwords into source control.
- Anton stores the string encrypted at rest, but treat it like a password.
- Named instance syntax: `Server=myhost\SQLEXPRESS,1433;`

fields.name: connection_string
fields.label: Connection String
fields.description: Full ADO.NET connection string. Paste exactly as provided — Anton will parse host, port, database, and credentials from it.
